questionText: The heights, $X$ cm, of adult males in a region are modelled by $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. It is found that $15\%$ of men are shorter than $160$ cm and $10\%$ are taller than $185$ cm. (a) Find $\mu$ and $\sigma$ to $3$ significant figures. (b) Using your values, find the proportion of men with height between $165$ cm and $180$ cm.

steps.description: State the model and the two given facts
steps.explanation: Heights are normal with unknown $\mu$ and $\sigma$. ‘$15\%$ shorter than $160$’ gives $P(X<160)=0.15$ and ‘$10\%$ taller than $185$’ gives $P(X>185)=0.10$. These two proportions fix the two parameters.
Standardising with $Z=\dfrac{X-\mu}{\sigma}$ turns the height $160$ into $\dfrac{160-\mu}{\sigma}$, keeping the lower-tail area at $0.15$.
With only $15\%$ of the area below it, this standardised value lies left of the mean and is therefore negative.
Equating the standardised value of $160$ with $z_1$ gives the first equation linking $\mu$ and $\sigma$.

steps.description: Convert and standardise the second condition
steps.explanation: Since $10\%$ are taller than $185$, exactly $90\%$ are shorter; converting to the lower tail lets us apply the inverse normal after standardising $185$.
The value with $90\%$ of the area below it is positive; the inverse normal gives $z_2 = 1.2816$.
Equating the standardised value of $185$ with $z_2$ completes the simultaneous pair.

steps.description: Subtract the equations (cleared of fractions) to eliminate mu
steps.explanation: Writing each as ‘value $-\ \mu = z\sigma$’ and subtracting removes $\mu$. The $25$ cm gap in heights matches a gap of $2.318$ standard deviations.
Dividing the height gap by the standardised gap gives the standard deviation, about $10.8$ cm.
From the first equation $\mu = 160 + 1.0364\,\sigma$. The mean height of about $171$ cm sits comfortably between the two quoted heights.
To three significant figures the model is $X \sim N(171,\ 10.8^2)$ cm; these values answer part (a).

steps.description: Set up part (b) and standardise both limits
steps.explanation: For part (b) we need the area between two heights, so we standardise each limit using the parameters just found. This converts the question into a standard-normal interval.
The lower height $165$ is just below the mean, giving a small negative $z$, while $180$ is above the mean, giving a positive $z$.

steps.description: Write the probability as a difference of Phi values
steps.explanation: The area between two points is the cumulative probability up to the upper limit minus that up to the lower limit; the calculator supplies each $\Phi$ value.
So about $50.8\%$ of men have a height between $165$ cm and $180$ cm — roughly half the population, which is reasonable since this band straddles the mean.
